points.text: Enable request tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to see latency per downstream call.
Correlate 502/504 spikes with DB CPU and query duration metrics.
Identify slow or failing downstream services via timeout and error counters.
Add or tighten per-call timeouts and circuit breakers to fail fast.
Profile DB queries; add indexes or optimize heavy queries causing CPU load.
Scale DB read replicas or increase connection pool size if saturation observed.
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries and ensure idempotent operations.
Enable distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to capture per‑request latency of each downstream call.
Correlate gateway 502/504 logs with trace IDs to see which service timed out or returned error.
Instrument DB query latency and CPU; identify slow queries or connection pool exhaustion.
Run a load test against the backend to reproduce the CPU spike and measure downstream call durations.
Check circuit‑breaker settings; ensure they open on repeated timeouts to protect the gateway.
Profile the backend code to see if calls are serialized unnecessarily; consider parallelizing independent calls.
If DB load is high, add query indexes, optimize statements, or increase connection pool size.
Adjust timeout budgets: set per‑call timeouts lower than gateway timeout to fail fast and trigger retries.
Implement retry with exponential backoff and idempotency for transient downstream failures.
Monitor key metrics (CPU, latency, error rates) and set alerts to catch regressions early.
Enable pg_stat_statements to find the highest‑CPU queries.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on those queries to spot plan inefficiencies.
Add missing indexes on filter/join columns that cause sequential scans.
Check pg_locks for lock contention and terminate long‑running transactions.
Verify connection pool size; too many concurrent connections can overload CPU.
Instrument downstream calls; use timeouts/circuit breakers to avoid cascading delays.
Refactor queries to reduce result size, avoid N+1 patterns, and use proper joins.
Monitor OS metrics (CPU, context switches) to ensure the host isn’t saturated.
If CPU remains high, consider vertical scaling or adding read replicas.
Tune PostgreSQL settings (work_mem, max_parallel_workers_per_gather, effective_cache_size).
Enable DB monitoring (CPU, active sessions, lock waits, query stats) to get a baseline of resource usage.
Run a top‑CPU query report (e.g., pg_stat_statements) and EXPLAIN ANALYZE the hottest statements.
Inspect lock tables (pg_locks, wait events) for contention or deadlocks caused by recent changes.
Review recent schema or index changes that may have introduced full scans or inefficient plans.
Check connection pool size versus DB max connections to ensure the pool isn’t saturating the DB.
If reads dominate, consider read replicas or lower isolation levels to reduce lock pressure.
Verify load balancer health checks and error rates for backend instances.
Inspect LB request queue depth and max connections; watch for spikes matching 502/504.
Compare gateway timeout settings with average backend latency; increase if needed.
Enable request tracing (e.g., X-Request-ID) to measure each downstream call's latency.
Collect backend metrics: CPU, memory, thread pool, DB connection pool usage during incidents.
Check DB performance: query latency, lock contention, CPU saturation; consider optimization or scaling.
Review kernel TCP stats (SYN retries, retransmits) for network‑level timeout clues.
Correlate error timestamps with resource exhaustion alerts (CPU, memory, socket limits).
Implement circuit breaker or retry backoff for downstream services to prevent cascades.
Temporarily scale out backend instances or add DB read replicas while investigating root cause.
Enable TCP metrics (retransmits, RTT, SYN‑retries) on gateway and backend via netstat/ss to spot congestion.
Capture packet traces (tcpdump/wireshark) between gateway and downstream services to detect loss or high latency.
Verify keepalive and timeout settings; increase if premature 502/504 are caused by idle‑connection drops.
Check backend connection‑pool sizes; pool exhaustion can cause gateway timeouts and 502/504 responses.
Correlate DB CPU spikes with network queue lengths; high CPU may delay replies, leading to gateway errors.
Run targeted load tests to isolate whether latency originates from network path or database processing.
Implement circuit‑breaker and exponential‑backoff retries to prevent cascading failures during spikes.
Measure end‑to‑end latency and break it into per‑dependency budgets; ensure each downstream timeout ≤ budget.
Apply Little’s Law (L = λ·W) to estimate needed DB connections given observed request rate λ and average DB service time W.
Compare observed DB CPU utilization to expected load; if CPU > 80% at current λ, consider scaling or query optimization.
Use p95/p99 latency of each downstream call to identify the slowest component; high tail latency often causes 502/504.
Gradually reduce concurrency or add circuit‑breaker limits on downstream calls to see if error rate drops, confirming overload.
